title: Present Simple & Continuous
blurb: Know when to use the simple present and when to use the present continuous.

lesson.body: The present simple describes facts, habits and routines, while the present continuous describes actions happening now or around now. Choosing the correct tense changes the meaning of a sentence.

lesson.title: Present Simple
lesson.body: Form: base verb (+ -s for he/she/it). Use it for facts (Water boils at 100°C), habits (She studies every evening) and permanent situations (He works in marketing). Add do/does for questions and negatives: She doesn't study on Sundays.

lesson.title: Present Continuous
lesson.body: Form: am/is/are + verb-ing. Use it for actions happening at this moment (The graph is showing a decline), temporary situations (He is living abroad this year) and current trends (Prices are rising).

lesson.title: Stative verbs
lesson.body: Some verbs describe states, not actions, and are rarely used in the continuous form: know, believe, want, own, understand, prefer. Say I understand the question, not I am understanding.

head: Use
Tense
Example
rows: The sun rises in the east.
She checks her essays before submitting.
The number of applicants is increasing.
He is studying at a language centre this term.

items.en: The table shows that inflation rises every winter.
items.note: General fact/pattern — present simple.

items.en: Look at the graph: the line is climbing sharply after 2015.
items.note: Describing what is happening in the visual right now — present continuous.

items.en: I understand your argument, but I disagree with the conclusion.
items.note: "Understand" is a stative verb — no continuous form.

items.en: More students are choosing to study abroad this year.
items.note: A changing, temporary trend — present continuous.

items.en: The report states that unemployment affects young people the most.
items.note: General fact about the report's content — present simple.

lesson.body: In Writing Task 1, use the present simple for the chart's overall pattern (The graph shows…) and the present continuous only for a trend you are describing as still in progress at the final data point (the figure is still rising in 2020).

quiz.prompt: Water ___ at 100°C at sea level.
quiz.why: A scientific fact takes the present simple, with -s added for "it".

quiz.prompt: Look! The number of visitors ___ rapidly this month.
quiz.why: An action in progress right now takes the present continuous.

quiz.prompt: She usually ___ (study) in the library after class.
quiz.why: A habitual action takes the present simple, with -s for "she".

quiz.prompt: I ___ what you mean, but I still think the plan is risky.
quiz.why: "Understand" is a stative verb and is not used in the continuous form.

quiz.prompt: At the moment, more graduates ___ (choose) careers in technology.
quiz.why: A current, changing trend takes the present continuous.

quiz.prompt: He ___ to the office by train every day.
quiz.why: A daily routine takes the present simple; add -s for "he".

quiz.prompt: ___ she work on weekends?
quiz.why: Present simple questions with she/he/it use "does".

quiz.prompt: The chart ___ (show) a steady decline between 2005 and 2010.
quiz.why: Describing the general content of a chart uses the present simple.

quiz.prompt: He ___ (not/like) working overtime, but he does it anyway.
quiz.why: Present simple negative for he/she/it uses "doesn't" + base verb.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite in the present continuous to describe a trend visible right now on the graph: "The population increases every year."
answers: The population is increasing every year.
The population is increasing every year
quiz.why: A trend described as currently happening takes the present continuous.

title: Past Simple & Continuous
blurb: Narrate finished actions and describe interrupted or background actions in the past.

lesson.body: The past simple describes completed actions at a definite time in the past, while the past continuous describes an action in progress — often interrupted by another action or used to set the scene.

lesson.title: Past Simple
lesson.body: Form: verb + -ed (regular) or an irregular form (went, wrote, saw). Use it for finished actions with a stated or implied time: The survey collected data in 2019.

lesson.title: Past Continuous
lesson.body: Form: was/were + verb-ing. Use it for an action in progress at a specific past moment (Respondents were still completing the form when the deadline passed) or to describe the background to another event.

lesson.title: Combining the two
lesson.body: Use while/as + past continuous for the longer background action and the past simple for the shorter action that interrupted it: While researchers were analysing the data, a new trend emerged.

head: Use
Tense
Example
rows: Action in progress at a past moment
At 9 a.m. the team was still collecting responses.
While prices were falling, demand suddenly rose.

items.en: Researchers surveyed 500 participants last spring.
items.note: A completed action with a stated time — past simple.

items.en: While the economy was recovering, unemployment continued to fall.
items.note: Background action in progress — past continuous.

items.en: The figure was rising steadily until it suddenly dropped in March.
items.note: Trend in progress, interrupted by a sudden change.

items.en: She wrote her dissertation while she was working part-time.
items.note: A completed main action alongside a background action.

items.en: They collected the data before the policy changed.
items.note: Two completed, sequential past actions — both past simple.

lesson.body: In Task 1 line graphs, use the past continuous for a trend that was still developing at a point in time (the figure was falling until 2015) and the past simple for a single completed change (it fell sharply in 2016).

quiz.prompt: The number of applicants ___ sharply last year.
quiz.why: A completed change with a stated past time takes the past simple.

quiz.prompt: While researchers ___ the results, they noticed an error.
quiz.why: The background action in progress takes the past continuous.

quiz.prompt: She ___ (write) her report when the fire alarm rang.
quiz.why: An action interrupted mid-progress takes the past continuous.

quiz.prompt: They ___ the survey in 2020.
quiz.why: A finished action with a definite past time takes the past simple.

quiz.prompt: At 8 p.m. yesterday, the committee ___ (discuss) the proposal.
quiz.why: An action in progress at a specific past time takes the past continuous.

quiz.prompt: The line ___ steadily before it suddenly dropped in March.
quiz.why: A trend still in progress before an interruption takes the past continuous.

quiz.prompt: He ___ (not/attend) the seminar because he was ill.
quiz.why: Past simple negative uses "didn't" + base verb.

quiz.prompt: ___ you see the announcement yesterday?
quiz.why: Past simple questions use "did" + base verb.

quiz.prompt: While the population ___ (grow), housing prices increased at an even faster rate.
quiz.why: A background trend in progress takes the past continuous.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite using the past continuous and past simple together, showing one action interrupting the other: "The team analysed the results. At the same time, a new report was published."
answers: While the team was analysing the results, a new report was published.
While the team was analysing the results, a new report was published
quiz.why: The longer background action takes the past continuous; the shorter completed action takes the past simple.

title: Future Forms (will / going to)
blurb: Choose between will, going to and the present continuous for future plans and predictions.

lesson.body: English has several ways to talk about the future. The three most common for learners are will, going to, and the present continuous — each suits a different kind of future meaning.

lesson.title: Will
lesson.body: Use will + base verb for spontaneous decisions (I'll answer that email now), predictions based on opinion (I think the trend will continue), and promises or offers.

lesson.title: Going to
lesson.body: Use am/is/are + going to + base verb for plans already decided before speaking (We are going to launch the survey next month) and for predictions based on present evidence (Look at the data — the figure is going to fall further).

lesson.title: Present continuous for arrangements
lesson.body: Use the present continuous for fixed future arrangements with a time and place already set: She is meeting the supervisor on Friday.

head: Use
Form
Example
rows: I think the figure will rise next year.
We are going to redesign the survey.
They are presenting their findings tomorrow.

items.en: Analysts believe the trend will continue into next year.
items.note: An opinion-based prediction — will.

items.en: Based on current data, the figure is going to exceed last year's peak.
items.note: A prediction based on present evidence — going to.

items.en: The committee is meeting on Thursday to review the results.
items.note: A fixed, pre-arranged future event — present continuous.

items.en: I'll send you the final report as soon as it is ready.
items.note: A spontaneous offer made at the moment of speaking — will.

items.en: The university is going to introduce a new grading system next term.
items.note: A decision already made before speaking — going to.

lesson.body: In Task 2 essays, use will for cautious, general predictions about the future (this trend will likely continue) rather than going to, which sounds too informal for academic writing.

quiz.prompt: Look at those clouds — it ___ rain soon.
quiz.why: Present evidence supports a "going to" prediction.

quiz.prompt: I think renewable energy use ___ increase over the next decade.
quiz.why: A general opinion-based prediction uses "will".

quiz.prompt: We ___ (present) our findings at the conference next week — it's already booked.
quiz.why: A fixed future arrangement takes the present continuous.

quiz.prompt: A: The phone's ringing. B: Don't worry, I ___ it.
quiz.why: A spontaneous decision made at the moment of speaking uses "will".

quiz.prompt: The company ___ (launch) a new product line — the plan was approved last month.
quiz.why: A plan decided before the moment of speaking takes "going to".

quiz.prompt: Based on the current data, house prices ___ rise further next quarter.
quiz.why: The prediction is based on visible present evidence, so "going to" is used.

quiz.prompt: She ___ (meet) her supervisor at 10 a.m. tomorrow — it's already in the diary.
quiz.why: A fixed arrangement with a set time takes the present continuous.

quiz.prompt: I promise I ___ finish the assignment by Friday.
quiz.why: Promises are expressed with "will".

quiz.prompt: The results ___ (not/be) ready until next Monday.
quiz.why: Future negative with "will" forms "won't".

quiz.prompt: Rewrite as a prediction based on present evidence: "Sales figures are falling. They will drop below target."
answers: Sales figures are falling. They are going to drop below target.
Sales figures are falling. They are going to drop below target
quiz.why: A prediction based on visible current evidence uses "going to", not "will".

title: Question Forms & Short Answers
blurb: Form yes/no and wh- questions correctly, give short answers, and use imperatives for instructions.

lesson.body: Questions in English usually need an auxiliary verb (do/does/did, am/is/are, have/has, will) before the subject. Imperatives, used for instructions and commands, drop the subject entirely.

lesson.title: Yes/No questions
lesson.body: Invert the auxiliary and subject: Do you agree?, Is the trend continuing?, Have they submitted the report? Answer briefly with the same auxiliary: Yes, I do. / No, it isn't.

lesson.title: Wh- questions
lesson.body: Put the question word first, then the auxiliary, then the subject: Why did the figure fall? What is the chart showing? When the question word is the subject, no auxiliary is added: Who wrote this report?

lesson.title: Imperatives
lesson.body: Use the base verb with no subject for instructions, advice or commands: Look at the graph. Do not copy the source text word for word. Add please to sound more polite.

head: Type
Pattern
Example
rows: Which country produced the most steel?
Compare the two graphs before you write.

items.en: Does the graph show a rise or a fall in exports?
items.note: Yes/No question with "does" for a singular subject.

items.en: Why did unemployment increase so sharply in 2020?
items.note: Wh- question with "did" before the subject.

items.en: Which sector employed the most workers?
items.note: "Which sector" is the subject, so no extra auxiliary is needed.

items.en: Read the instructions carefully before you begin the test.
items.note: An imperative giving an instruction — no subject.

items.en: Have the researchers published their conclusions yet?
items.note: Yes/No question with "have" for the present perfect.

lesson.body: Task 1 instructions are imperatives (Summarise the information… Write at least 150 words) — read them carefully, since they tell you exactly what the examiner expects.

quiz.prompt: ___ the survey include international students?
quiz.why: A yes/no question about a singular subject in the present simple uses "does".

quiz.prompt: ___ caused the sudden drop in sales?
quiz.why: "What" is the subject of the question, so no extra auxiliary is added.

quiz.prompt: ___ you finished the report yet?
quiz.why: A present perfect yes/no question begins with "have" before the subject.

quiz.prompt: A: Did they attend the seminar? B: Yes, ___.
quiz.why: Short answers repeat the auxiliary used in the question — "did".

quiz.prompt: ___ (not/write) your answers in pencil — use blue or black ink.
quiz.why: A negative imperative begins with "do not" or "don't" + base verb.

quiz.prompt: ___ country has the highest population density?
quiz.why: "Which country" is the subject of the question, so no auxiliary is needed.

quiz.prompt: ___ the figures rise or fall between 2015 and 2020?
quiz.why: A past simple yes/no question begins with "did" before the subject.

quiz.prompt: ___ carefully before you answer the questions.
quiz.why: An imperative uses the base form of the verb with no subject.

quiz.prompt: A: Is the government planning new policies? B: No, it ___.
quiz.why: Short negative answers repeat the auxiliary from the question.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite as a wh- question asking about the time: "The report was published in March."
quiz.why: A wh- question about time begins with "when", followed by the auxiliary and subject.

title: Word Forms (noun/verb/adj/adv)
blurb: Recognise and build noun, verb, adjective and adverb forms of the same word family.

lesson.body: Many English words share a root but change form depending on their job in the sentence: a noun names a thing, a verb shows an action, an adjective describes a noun, and an adverb describes a verb, adjective or another adverb.

lesson.title: Common suffixes
lesson.body: Nouns often end in -tion, -ment, -ness, -ity (education, development, happiness, popularity); adjectives often end in -ful, -ive, -al, -ous (successful, effective, national, dangerous); adverbs are usually adjective + -ly (successfully, effectively).

lesson.title: Choosing the right form
lesson.body: Look at the word's job: after a/the or as a subject/object, use a noun (the rise in prices); before a noun or after be, use an adjective (a significant rise); to modify a verb, use an adverb (prices rose significantly).

items.en: The government's decision to increase funding was announced yesterday.
items.note: "Decision" is the noun form, used as the subject.

items.en: Researchers decided to repeat the experiment.
items.note: "Decided" is the verb form.

items.en: The results were statistically significant.
items.note: "Significant" is the adjective form, describing "results".

items.en: The figure rose significantly between 2015 and 2018.
items.note: "Significantly" is the adverb form, modifying the verb "rose".

items.en: Her successful application impressed the admissions panel.
items.note: "Successful" is the adjective, describing "application".

lesson.body: In Task 2, examiners reward accurate word forms. Before submitting, check every key word: does the sentence need a noun, verb, adjective or adverb? A wrong form can cost marks even if the meaning is clear.

quiz.prompt: The company's ___ has grown steadily over the past decade. (SUCCEED)
quiz.why: After "the company's", a noun is needed, not a verb or adjective.

quiz.prompt: The government hopes to ___ (REDUCE) unemployment next year.
quiz.why: After "to", the base verb form is required.

quiz.prompt: The survey produced ___ results. (SIGNIFICANCE)
quiz.why: Before the noun "results", an adjective is needed.

quiz.prompt: House prices rose ___ (SIGNIFICANCE) in the final quarter.
quiz.why: An adverb is needed to modify the verb "rose".

quiz.prompt: Her application was ___, and she was offered the position. (SUCCEED)
quiz.why: After the verb "be", an adjective describes the subject.

quiz.prompt: The team's ___ (DECIDE) to expand overseas surprised investors.
quiz.why: After "the team's", a noun is needed.

quiz.prompt: Researchers ___ to test the hypothesis again. (DECIDE)
quiz.why: A finite verb form is needed after the subject "researchers".

quiz.prompt: She responded ___ (DECIDE) when questioned about the results.
quiz.why: An adverb is needed to modify the verb "responded".

quiz.prompt: ___ is essential for long-term economic growth. (EDUCATE)
quiz.why: As the subject of the sentence, a noun is required.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite with the correct word forms: "The company announced a significant expand, and sales grew successful."
answers: The company announced a significant expansion, and sales grew successfully.
The company announced a significant expansion, and sales grew successfully
quiz.why: "Expand" (verb) should be the noun "expansion"; "successful" (adjective) should be the adverb "successfully" to modify "grew".

title: Articles (a/an/the)
blurb: Choose between a, an, the — or no article at all.

lesson.body: Articles are small words with a big job: they tell the reader whether a noun is one of many (a city) or a specific one (the city we visited). English has three choices: a/an, the, and no article at all.

lesson.title: A or An
lesson.body: Use a before a consonant sound and an before a vowel sound. It is the sound that matters, not the letter: a university (/j/ sound), an hour (silent h).

lesson.title: The
lesson.body: Use the when both speaker and listener know which one: something already mentioned (a graph… the graph shows), unique things (the internet, the environment), and superlatives (the largest city).

lesson.title: No article
lesson.body: Use no article for plural or uncountable nouns in general statements: Cities are growing, Education is important — very common in IELTS essay openings.

items.en: The graph shows an increase in car ownership.
items.note: "increase" begins with a vowel sound — an.

items.en: A minority of respondents disagreed, and the majority supported the plan.
items.note: First mention takes a; the known group takes the.

items.en: Unemployment rose sharply in the 1990s.
items.note: Uncountable noun in a general statement — no article; decades take the.

items.en: She attends a university in the largest city in Vietnam.
items.note: a + consonant sound /j/; superlative takes the.

items.en: Governments should invest in public transport.
items.note: General plural statement — no article.

lesson.body: In Writing Task 1, your first sentence usually introduces the visual with the: The chart illustrates…. But trends inside it are often first mentions: there was a sharp rise…. Mixing these up is one of the most common band-lowering slips.

quiz.prompt: She is ___ engineer at a software company.
quiz.why: "Engineer" begins with a vowel sound, so we use "an".

quiz.prompt: He graduated from ___ university in Hanoi.
quiz.why: "University" starts with a /j/ (consonant) sound, so "a" is correct.

quiz.prompt: The chart shows ___ number of visitors between 2010 and 2020.
quiz.why: "The number of" is a fixed, specific reference — it takes "the".

quiz.prompt: ___ pollution is a serious problem in many cities.
quiz.why: Uncountable noun in a general statement — no article.

quiz.prompt: There was ___ sharp increase in exports, and the increase continued until 2019.
quiz.why: First mention of the increase is one of many — "a"; the second mention takes "the".

quiz.prompt: It took her ___ hour to finish the reading section.
quiz.why: The "h" in "hour" is silent, so the word begins with a vowel sound.

quiz.prompt: Ho Chi Minh City is ___ largest city in Vietnam.
quiz.why: Superlatives always take "the".

quiz.prompt: ___ internet has transformed how students prepare for exams.
quiz.why: Unique, one-of-a-kind things take "the".

quiz.prompt: In general, ___ children learn languages faster than adults.
quiz.why: General plural statement — no article is used.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite with the correct articles: "Graph shows increase in number of tourists."
answers: The graph shows an increase in the number of tourists.
The graph shows an increase in the number of tourists
quiz.why: The known visual takes "the"; a first-mention rise takes "an"; "the number of" is fixed.

title: Nouns, Plurals & Countability
blurb: Form regular and irregular plurals, and distinguish countable from uncountable nouns.

lesson.body: Nouns can be countable (you can count them: one chart, two charts) or uncountable (you cannot count them directly: information, research). Countable nouns have singular and plural forms; uncountable nouns do not.

lesson.title: Regular and irregular plurals
lesson.body: Most nouns add -s (graphs, students) or -es after s/x/ch/sh (boxes, churches). Nouns ending in a consonant + -y change to -ies (country → countries). Some plurals are irregular: child → children, person → people, datum → data.

lesson.title: Countable vs uncountable
lesson.body: Uncountable nouns (information, advice, research, furniture, equipment) have no plural and take a singular verb: The information is useful, not informations. Use a piece of / an item of to count them: a piece of advice.

items.en: The researchers analysed several surveys from different countries.
items.note: Regular plurals: surveys, and "country" → "countries".

items.en: Very little information was available about the earlier study.
items.note: "Information" is uncountable and takes a singular verb.

items.en: The data show a clear upward trend.
items.note: "Data" is traditionally plural in formal academic English.

items.en: She was given a useful piece of advice before the interview.
items.note: "Advice" is uncountable, so "a piece of" is used to count it.

items.en: Many children in the study were tested twice.
items.note: "Child" has the irregular plural "children".

lesson.body: Common IELTS uncountable nouns include information, advice, research, evidence, equipment, furniture, homework, news. Never add -s to these, and always pair them with a singular verb.

quiz.prompt: The report contains useful ___ about local businesses.
quiz.why: "Information" is uncountable and never takes a plural -s.

quiz.prompt: Several ___ (COUNTRY) took part in the international survey.
quiz.why: "Country" ends in consonant + y, so the plural is "countries".

quiz.prompt: The ___ collected so far support the initial hypothesis. (DATA)
quiz.why: "Data" is the standard plural form used with a plural verb in academic writing.

quiz.prompt: The ___ (CHILD) in the study were tested at two different ages.
quiz.why: "Child" has the irregular plural "children".

quiz.prompt: We need new ___ for the laboratory. (EQUIPMENT)
quiz.why: "Equipment" is uncountable and has no plural form.

quiz.prompt: The museum has several ___ (BOX) of archived documents.
quiz.why: Nouns ending in -x add -es to form the plural.

quiz.prompt: She gave me a useful ___ of advice before the exam. (PIECE)
quiz.why: Uncountable nouns like "advice" are counted with "a piece of".

quiz.prompt: The ___ (PERSON) interviewed for the study came from different backgrounds.
quiz.why: "Person" has the irregular plural "people".

quiz.prompt: Very little ___ has been done on this topic. (RESEARCH)
quiz.why: "Research" is uncountable and takes a singular verb, with no plural form.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite correcting the noun forms: "The childs gave many informations about their familys."
answers: The children gave a lot of information about their families.
The children gave lots of information about their families.
quiz.why: "Child" becomes "children"; "information" is uncountable so it takes no plural; "family" becomes "families".

title: Quantifiers & There is/are
blurb: Use much, many, a lot of, few and there is/are accurately with countable and uncountable nouns.

lesson.body: Quantifiers tell us how much or how many of something there is. The right choice depends on whether the noun is countable (books) or uncountable (money), and whether the sentence is a statement, question or negative.

lesson.title: Much, many, a lot of
lesson.body: Use many with plural countable nouns (many students) and much with uncountable nouns (much time), usually in questions and negatives. A lot of / lots of work with both, in any sentence type: a lot of information, a lot of respondents.

lesson.title: Few, little, some, any
lesson.body: Use a few (some, a small positive amount) and few (almost none, negative) with countable nouns; a little and little with uncountable nouns. Use some in positive statements and offers, any in negatives and most questions.

lesson.title: There is / there are
lesson.body: Use there is/was with singular or uncountable nouns and there are/were with plural nouns to say that something exists: There is a sharp rise in 2019. There are three main reasons for this.

head: Quantifier
Used with
Example
rows: a lot of information, a lot of students
there is one factor; there are two reasons

items.en: There are several reasons for the decline in sales.
items.note: "Reasons" is plural, so "there are" is used.

items.en: There is not much evidence to support this claim.
items.note: "Evidence" is uncountable, so "much" and "there is" are used.

items.en: Only a few participants completed the follow-up survey.
items.note: "A few" (a small positive number) with the countable noun "participants".

items.en: Little research has been carried out on this specific issue.
items.note: "Little" (almost none) with the uncountable noun "research".

items.en: A lot of graduates struggle to find relevant jobs immediately.
items.note: "A lot of" works with the countable plural noun "graduates".

lesson.body: In Task 2, avoid overusing "a lot of" — it is informal. Prefer "a significant number of" (countable) or "a considerable amount of" (uncountable) for a more academic tone.

quiz.prompt: There weren't ___ people at the lecture — the room was half empty.
quiz.why: "People" is countable and plural, so "many" is used in this negative sentence.

quiz.prompt: There ___ a sharp increase in exports in 2018.
quiz.why: "Increase" is singular, and the sentence refers to a specific past year, so "there was" is correct.

quiz.prompt: There ___ (be) three main causes of the problem, according to the report.
quiz.why: "Causes" is plural, so "there are" is used.

quiz.prompt: We don't have ___ time left before the deadline.
quiz.why: "Time" is uncountable, so "much" is used in this negative sentence.

quiz.prompt: Only ___ (FEW) students opposed the new policy — most supported it.
quiz.why: "Students" is countable, and "a few" here means a small positive number.

quiz.prompt: ___ research has been done on this rare condition, so little is known.
quiz.why: "Research" is uncountable, and "little" conveys almost none.

quiz.prompt: There ___ (not/be) enough evidence to draw a firm conclusion.
quiz.why: "Evidence" is uncountable and singular, so the negative form is "isn't".

quiz.prompt: ___ of the respondents said they were satisfied with the service.
quiz.why: "A lot" combines with the following "of the respondents"; adding "of" again would wrongly duplicate the preposition.

quiz.prompt: How ___ (much/many) information do we need before submitting the report?
quiz.why: "Information" is uncountable, so "much" is used in questions about quantity.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite correcting the quantifiers: "There is many reason for the delay, and we don't have much problems left to solve."
answers: There are many reasons for the delay, and we don't have many problems left to solve.
There are many reasons for the delay, and we do not have many problems left to solve.
quiz.why: "Reasons" and "problems" are countable plural nouns, so they need "there are" and "many", not "there is" and "much".

title: Pronouns & Possessives
blurb: Use subject, object, possessive and reflexive pronouns accurately.

lesson.body: Pronouns replace nouns to avoid repetition. English distinguishes subject pronouns (I, you, he, she, it, we, they), object pronouns (me, you, him, her, it, us, them), possessives (my/mine, your/yours…) and reflexives (myself, yourself…).

lesson.title: Subject vs object
lesson.body: Use a subject pronoun before the verb (She wrote the report) and an object pronoun after a verb or preposition (The teacher gave her feedback; the results surprised them).

lesson.title: Possessive adjectives vs possessive pronouns
lesson.body: A possessive adjective (my, your, his, her, its, our, their) always comes before a noun: her research. A possessive pronoun (mine, yours, his, hers, ours, theirs) stands alone, replacing the noun: This research is hers.

lesson.title: Reflexive pronouns
lesson.body: Use a reflexive pronoun (myself, yourself, himself, herself, itself, ourselves, yourselves, themselves) when the subject and object of the verb are the same person or thing: She taught herself to code.

items.en: The researchers published their findings, and they were widely cited.
items.note: "Their" is the possessive adjective before "findings"; "they" is the subject pronoun.

items.en: The professor gave him detailed feedback on his essay.
items.note: "Him" is the object pronoun; "his" is the possessive adjective before "essay".

items.en: This laptop is mine, not hers.
items.note: "Mine" and "hers" are possessive pronouns standing alone.

items.en: Students should check their work themselves before submitting it.
items.note: "Themselves" is reflexive because the subject and object refer to the same people.

items.en: The company increased its market share last year.
items.note: "Its" is the possessive form of "it", with no apostrophe.

lesson.body: Do not confuse its (possessive) with it's (it is/it has). In formal IELTS writing, "it's" should almost never appear — check every "its" for an accidental apostrophe.

quiz.prompt: The manager gave ___ the results before the meeting.
quiz.why: An object pronoun is needed after the verb "gave".

quiz.prompt: ___ (SHE) research focuses on renewable energy.
quiz.why: A possessive adjective is needed before the noun "research".

quiz.prompt: This report is not yours — it's ___.
quiz.why: A possessive pronoun stands alone, without a following noun.

quiz.prompt: The company must protect ___ (IT) reputation carefully.
quiz.why: "Its" is the possessive form of "it", used before the noun "reputation".

quiz.prompt: The students taught ___ to use the new software.
quiz.why: A reflexive pronoun is needed because the subject and object refer to the same people.

quiz.prompt: ___ (THEY) findings contradicted the earlier study.
quiz.why: A possessive adjective is needed before the noun "findings".

quiz.prompt: Between you and ___, I think the second option is stronger.
quiz.why: After a preposition ("between"), an object pronoun is required.

quiz.prompt: The idea was originally ___ (I), although she developed it further.
quiz.why: A possessive pronoun stands alone, replacing "my idea".

quiz.prompt: She completed the whole project by ___.
quiz.why: "By herself" is a fixed reflexive phrase meaning "alone".

quiz.prompt: Rewrite correcting the pronouns: "Me and him submitted our report, and the results surprised we."
answers: He and I submitted our report, and the results surprised us.
He and I submitted our report and the results surprised us.
quiz.why: Subject pronouns ("he", "I") are needed before the verb, and the object pronoun "us" is needed after "surprised".

title: Comparatives & Superlatives
blurb: Form and use comparative and superlative adjectives accurately, including irregular forms.

lesson.body: Comparatives compare two things (higher than), and superlatives identify the extreme within a group of three or more (the highest). The form depends on the length of the adjective.

lesson.title: Short adjectives
lesson.body: One-syllable adjectives, or two syllables ending in -y, add -er/-est: high → higher → the highest; busy → busier → the busiest (y changes to i). Double the final consonant after a short vowel: big → bigger → the biggest.

lesson.title: Long adjectives
lesson.body: Adjectives of two or more syllables use more/most: expensive → more expensive → the most expensive; popular → more popular → the most popular.

lesson.title: Irregular forms
lesson.body: Some common adjectives are irregular: good → better → the best; bad → worse → the worst; far → further/farther → the furthest/farthest. Comparatives are followed by than; superlatives usually take the.

items.en: The graph shows that urban populations grew faster than rural ones.
items.note: Short adjective + -er, followed by "than".

items.en: Country A recorded the highest levels of car ownership in the region.
items.note: Superlative of a short adjective, with "the".

items.en: This method is more efficient than the previous one.
items.note: Long adjective takes "more … than".

items.en: Overall, 2019 was the most successful year for exports.
items.note: Long adjective takes "the most" in the superlative.

items.en: The results were worse than expected, though not the worst on record.
items.note: Irregular comparative and superlative of "bad".

lesson.body: In Task 1, comparatives and superlatives help you highlight the most striking data points: the sharpest increase, the lowest figure, more than twice as high as. Vary these phrases instead of repeating "more" and "most" every sentence.

quiz.prompt: City A is ___ than City B in terms of population density. (BUSY)
quiz.why: "Busy" is a short adjective ending in -y, so it changes to "-ier" for the comparative.

quiz.prompt: This course is ___ (DIFFICULT) than the previous one.
quiz.why: "Difficult" has three syllables, so it takes "more" for the comparative.

quiz.prompt: Of all the regions, the north recorded ___ rainfall. (LOW, superlative)
quiz.why: "Low" is a short adjective, so the superlative adds "-est" with "the".

quiz.prompt: Overall, this was ___ (GOOD) result the team had ever achieved.
quiz.why: "Good" is irregular; its superlative is "the best".

quiz.prompt: The new policy proved ___ than expected. (EFFECTIVE)
quiz.why: "Effective" has three syllables, so the comparative uses "more … than".

quiz.prompt: Traffic congestion is ___ (BAD) in the capital than in smaller towns.
quiz.why: "Bad" is irregular; its comparative form is "worse".

quiz.prompt: This is ___ significant finding of the whole study. (superlative)
quiz.why: "Significant" is a long adjective, so its superlative is formed with "the most".

quiz.prompt: Of the two applicants, she performed ___ (WELL) in the interview.
quiz.why: "Well" corresponds to the irregular comparative "better".

quiz.prompt: The 2020 figures were ___ than analysts had predicted. (HIGH)
quiz.why: "High" is a short adjective, so its comparative is formed with "-er".

quiz.prompt: Rewrite as one comparative sentence: "Country A's economy grew fast. Country B's economy grew faster than that."
answers: Country B's economy grew faster than Country A's.
Country B's economy grew faster than Country A's economy.
quiz.why: A comparative adjective + "than" combines the two ideas into a single comparison.

title: Prepositions of Time & Place
blurb: Use in, on, at and other common prepositions correctly for time and place.

lesson.body: Prepositions of time and place tell us when and where something happens. The three most important — in, on, at — follow fairly predictable patterns, from the largest unit of time or space to the smallest.

lesson.title: Time: in, on, at
lesson.body: Use in for months, years, decades and centuries (in 2020, in the 1990s); on for days and dates (on Monday, on 5 July); at for clock times and certain fixed phrases (at 9 a.m., at night, at the weekend).

lesson.title: Place: in, on, at
lesson.body: Use in for enclosed spaces and larger areas (in the classroom, in Vietnam); on for surfaces and lines (on the table, on the graph); at for specific points (at the station, at the top of the page).

lesson.title: Other common prepositions
lesson.body: Between … and … compares two points; from … to … shows a range or duration; during shows something happening within a period: Sales rose between 2015 and 2018; the figure fell during the recession.

items.en: The number of visitors peaked in July 2019.
items.note: "In" is used with a month and year.

items.en: The meeting is scheduled for 3 p.m. on Friday.
items.note: "At" for the clock time; "on" for the day.

items.en: The line on the graph rises sharply between 2010 and 2015.
items.note: "On" for the surface of the graph; "between … and …" for the two points compared.

items.en: There was a steady decline during the recession.
items.note: "During" describes something happening within a period of time.

items.en: She studies at a university in the capital city.
items.note: "At" for the specific institution; "in" for the larger place, the city.

lesson.body: Task 1 answers often need "between X and Y" (for two compared points) versus "from X to Y" (for a duration or range) — check which one your sentence needs before you write it.

quiz.prompt: The exam takes place ___ 9 a.m. ___ Monday morning.
quiz.why: Clock times take "at"; days take "on".

quiz.prompt: The report was published ___ March 2021.
quiz.why: Months and years take "in".

quiz.prompt: There was a sharp rise ___ the number of applicants ___ 2018 and 2020.
quiz.why: A rise "in" a quantity is compared "between" two points.

quiz.prompt: Costs increased steadily ___ the economic crisis.
quiz.why: "During" shows something happening within a stated period.

quiz.prompt: The lecture will begin ___ 9 a.m. sharp.
quiz.why: Clock times take "at": at 9 a.m., at noon, at midnight.

quiz.prompt: The data ___ the chart show a downward trend.
quiz.why: "On" is used for something appearing on a surface, such as a chart or graph.

quiz.prompt: She was born ___ 1998 and grew up ___ a small town.
quiz.why: Years take "in"; towns and larger places also take "in".

quiz.prompt: Numbers rose steadily ___ 2000 to 2010, more than doubling.
quiz.why: "From … to …" introduces a range or duration.

quiz.prompt: We usually go over the results ___ the weekend, when the office is quiet.
quiz.why: "At the weekend" is a fixed time phrase in British English.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite correcting the prepositions: "The study was conducted on 2019, and the results were published at March."
answers: The study was conducted in 2019, and the results were published in March.
The study was conducted in 2019 and the results were published in March.
quiz.why: Years and months take "in", not "on" or "at".

title: Adverbs & Word Order
blurb: Place adverbs of frequency, manner and degree correctly within a sentence.

lesson.body: Adverbs add extra information about a verb, adjective or another adverb — how, how often, or to what degree something happens. Their meaning depends on being placed in the correct position in the sentence.

lesson.title: Adverbs of manner and degree
lesson.body: Adverbs of manner (usually formed with -ly: carefully, significantly) usually go after the verb or object: The figure rose significantly. Adverbs of degree (very, quite, extremely) go before the adjective or adverb they modify: a very steep rise.

lesson.title: Adverbs of frequency
lesson.body: Adverbs of frequency (always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never) go before the main verb but after the verb "be": She often submits her work late; she is rarely late for class.

lesson.title: Word order in sentences
lesson.body: The usual English order is Subject – Verb – Object – Manner – Place – Time: She wrote her essay carefully in the library yesterday. Moving the time expression to the front is also possible for emphasis: Yesterday, she wrote her essay carefully in the library.

head: Adverb type
Position
Example
rows: She rarely misses a deadline; she is always punctual.

items.en: The government has consistently invested in public transport.
items.note: Adverb of manner placed after the auxiliary and before the main verb.

items.en: Researchers rarely agree on the exact causes of the decline.
items.note: Adverb of frequency placed before the main verb.

items.en: The results were extremely encouraging for the research team.
items.note: Adverb of degree placed before the adjective it modifies.

items.en: She carefully reviewed the data before submitting her essay.
items.note: Adverb of manner placed before the main verb for emphasis.

items.en: Prices increased gradually throughout the decade.
items.note: Adverb of manner after the verb, followed by the time expression.

lesson.body: In Task 1, adverbs of degree describe the size of a change precisely: slightly, moderately, considerably, dramatically. Choosing the right one instead of always writing "a lot" raises the accuracy of your description.

quiz.prompt: She ___ checks her essays for grammar mistakes before submitting them. (ALWAYS)
quiz.why: Adverbs of frequency go before the main verb.

quiz.prompt: The company is ___ (RARE) late in delivering its quarterly reports.
quiz.why: Adverbs of frequency go after the verb "be".

quiz.prompt: The figures increased ___ over the final quarter. (SIGNIFICANCE, manner)
quiz.why: An adverb of manner is needed to modify the verb "increased".

quiz.prompt: The results were ___ (EXTREME) surprising to the research team.
quiz.why: An adverb of degree goes before the adjective it modifies.

quiz.prompt: ___, the report highlights three key recommendations.
quiz.why: "Overall" can begin the sentence as a summarising adverb, followed by a comma.

quiz.prompt: He ___ (CAREFUL) reviewed the data before drawing his conclusions.
quiz.why: An adverb of manner modifies the verb "reviewed".

quiz.prompt: She is ___ punctual, but yesterday she arrived late.
quiz.why: Adverbs of frequency go directly after the verb "be".

quiz.prompt: Costs rose ___ (GRADUAL) throughout the decade.
quiz.why: An adverb of manner describes how the costs rose.

quiz.prompt: The proposal was ___ unrealistic, according to most reviewers. (QUITE, degree)
quiz.why: An adverb of degree goes directly before the adjective it modifies.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite with the adverb in the correct position: "She reviewed careful the report before the meeting."
answers: She carefully reviewed the report before the meeting.
She reviewed the report carefully before the meeting.
quiz.why: The adverb of manner "carefully" should either precede the verb or follow the object, not interrupt the verb and its object.

title: Perfect Tenses
blurb: Link past, present and future with have/has/had + past participle — and their continuous forms.

lesson.body: Perfect tenses connect two points in time: an earlier action and a later reference point. The present perfect links the past to now, the past perfect links an earlier past to a later past, and the future perfect links an action to a point still to come.

lesson.title: Present Perfect
lesson.body: Use have/has + past participle for an unfinished time period (this year, so far), a life experience with no fixed time, or a past action with a present result. Common signals: already, yet, just, ever, never, since, for.

lesson.title: Past Perfect
lesson.body: Use had + past participle for the earlier of two past actions, to make the sequence clear: By the time the survey closed, researchers had collected over 500 responses.

lesson.title: Perfect Continuous & Future Perfect
lesson.body: The perfect continuous (have/had been + -ing) stresses the duration of an activity. The future perfect (will have + past participle) describes something completed before a future point: By 2030, the population will have doubled. The future perfect continuous (will have been + -ing) stresses duration up to that future point.

head: Tense
Form
Example
rows: Researchers have identified a clear trend.
The number had already fallen before the survey began.
By 2025, sales will have reached a record high.
Demand has been rising since January.

items.en: Researchers have found a strong link between diet and health.
items.note: Present perfect — a past action with a result relevant now.

items.en: By the time she arrived, the meeting had already started.
items.note: Past perfect — the earlier of two past events.

items.en: By 2040, the city will have built three new metro lines.
items.note: Future perfect — completed before a future point.

items.en: Unemployment has been falling steadily for the past two years.
items.note: Present perfect continuous — emphasises duration up to now.

items.en: He had been working at the company for a decade before he retired.
items.note: Past perfect continuous — duration before another past event.

lesson.body: In Writing Task 1, use the present perfect (has risen, have fluctuated) for a period running up to the present, and the future perfect (will have reached) for predictions on a graph that extends beyond today. Precise control of these forms is one marker examiners use for band 7+ Grammatical Range and Accuracy.

quiz.prompt: The team ___ three new products this month.
quiz.why: "This month" is an unfinished time period, so the present perfect (has launched) is required.

quiz.prompt: She already ___ (finish) the report, so she has some free time now.
quiz.why: "Already" with a present result signals the present perfect: has + past participle.

quiz.prompt: By the time the survey closed, researchers ___ over 500 responses.
quiz.why: Collecting the responses happened before another past event (the survey closing), so the past perfect is needed.

quiz.prompt: The plane ___ (already/leave) by the time we reached the airport.
quiz.why: The leaving happened before another past action (reaching the airport), so the past perfect is required.

quiz.prompt: Scientists predict that by 2050, global sea levels ___ by at least half a metre, and they will keep rising after that.
quiz.why: "By 2050" marks a completed milestone before the rise continues further, so the future perfect is correct.

quiz.prompt: By the time you read this letter, I ___ (already/leave) the country.
quiz.why: "By the time" + a future reference point requires the future perfect.

quiz.prompt: Your hands are covered in paint — ___ (you/paint) the fence?
quiz.why: The present perfect continuous links a recent activity to visible present evidence (the paint on the hands).

quiz.prompt: He was exhausted because he ___ (study) all night for the exam.
quiz.why: An activity leading up to another past state (exhaustion) can take the past perfect continuous or past perfect.

quiz.prompt: She ___ to Japan in 2019.
quiz.why: "In 2019" is a finished, specific past time, so the present perfect cannot be used — the simple past is correct.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite using the present perfect: "The last time I saw her was three years ago."
answers: I haven't seen her for three years.
I have not seen her for three years.
I haven't seen her for three years
I have not seen her for three years
quiz.why: The negative present perfect with "for" expresses time since a past action up to now.

title: Passive & Active Voice
blurb: Shift the focus from the doer to the action with be + past participle.

lesson.body: In the active voice, the subject performs the action (Workers built the bridge). In the passive voice, the subject receives the action (The bridge was built). English switches to the passive when the doer is unknown, unimportant, obvious, or simply less important than the action itself.

lesson.title: Forming the passive
lesson.body: Use be (in the tense you need) + past participle. The doer is optional and added with by only when it matters: The report was written (by three researchers).

lesson.title: When to use the passive
lesson.body: Choose the passive when the agent is unknown or unimportant, when describing a general process, or when a formal, objective tone is needed — very common in academic writing and IELTS Task 1 process diagrams.

lesson.title: Passive across tenses and modals
lesson.body: Almost every tense has a passive form: is produced (present simple), was built (past simple), has been completed (present perfect), will be launched (future), must be submitted (modal + be + past participle).

items.en: Steel is produced by heating iron ore in a furnace.
items.note: Present simple passive — describing a general process.

items.en: The bridge was completed in 1932.
items.note: Past simple passive — the builder is unimportant.

items.en: The results will be announced next Friday.
items.note: Future passive.

items.en: The proposal has already been approved by the committee.
items.note: Present perfect passive with a stated agent.

items.en: All applications must be submitted online.
items.note: Modal passive — an obligation about the process, not the doer.

lesson.body: Task 1 process diagrams are almost always described in the passive (the material is heated, then it is cooled) because no individual agent is relevant. In Speaking, however, overusing the passive can sound unnatural — balancing active and passive is part of band 7+ Grammatical Range and Accuracy.

quiz.prompt: The bridge ___ in 1932.
quiz.why: The builder is not mentioned and is unimportant, so the past simple passive (was built) is correct.

quiz.prompt: Steel ___ (produce) in large factories using this method.
quiz.why: A general process with no stated agent takes the present simple passive.

quiz.prompt: This report ___ by three researchers.
quiz.why: The focus is on the report, not the researchers, so the passive (was written) fits, with the agent added by "by".

quiz.prompt: The results ___ (announce) at tomorrow's press conference.
quiz.why: A future action with no important agent takes the future passive: will be + past participle.

quiz.prompt: All applications ___ submitted online before the deadline.
quiz.why: A modal passive needs modal + be + past participle: must be submitted.

quiz.prompt: The new policy ___ (already/implement) in several regions.
quiz.why: An unfinished time period with an unstated agent takes the present perfect passive.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite in the passive: "Someone stole my laptop last night."
answers: My laptop was stolen last night.
My laptop was stolen last night
quiz.why: The unknown agent ("someone") is dropped and the focus shifts to the laptop, the receiver of the action.

quiz.prompt: The museum ___ (visit) by thousands of tourists every year.
quiz.why: A general, repeated fact about the museum takes the present simple passive.

quiz.prompt: Which sentence best fits an academic description of a manufacturing process, where the person doing the action does not need to be mentioned?
quiz.why: Process descriptions in academic writing omit the agent and focus on the action, so the passive is preferred.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite in the passive: "The company will launch the product next month."
answers: The product will be launched next month.
The product will be launched next month
quiz.why: The future passive (will be + past participle) shifts focus from the company to the product.

title: Reported Speech
blurb: Report what people said with tense back-shift, and pronoun and time changes.

lesson.body: Reported (indirect) speech tells us what someone said without quoting their exact words. When the reporting verb is in the past (she said), the tense of the original words usually shifts one step back, and pronouns and time/place words change to fit the new speaker and moment.

lesson.title: Tense back-shift
lesson.body: Present simple → past simple; present continuous → past continuous; present perfect → past perfect; past simple → past perfect; will → would; can → could; must → had to.

lesson.title: Pronoun, time and place changes
lesson.body: I → he/she; my → his/her; now → then; today → that day; tomorrow → the next day; here → there; this → that.

lesson.title: Reporting questions and commands
lesson.body: Reported questions use statement word order (no auxiliary inversion, no question mark), with if/whether for yes/no questions or the wh-word for wh-questions. Reported commands and requests use told/asked + object + to-infinitive: She told me to close the door.

head: Direct speech
Reported speech
rows: He said (that) he would call me.
She asked if I liked coffee.
He told me to close the door.

items.en: She said (that) she was studying for her exam.
items.note: Present continuous shifts to past continuous.

items.en: He asked where I worked.
items.note: Reported wh-question — statement word order, no "do".

items.en: She asked if I lived nearby.
items.note: Reported yes/no question uses "if".

items.en: The manager told the staff to submit their reports by Friday.
items.note: Reported command with told + object + to-infinitive.

items.en: He said he would finish the project the next day.
items.note: "Will" → "would"; "tomorrow" → "the next day".

lesson.body: In Speaking Part 2 narrative tasks and in Writing when you summarise what a source said, accurate back-shift (especially remembering to change will to would) signals strong grammatical range. Forgetting the shift is one of the most common slips at this level.

quiz.prompt: Direct: "I am studying for my exam," she said. Reported: She said (that) she ___ for her exam.
quiz.why: The reporting verb "said" is past, so the present continuous shifts back to the past continuous.

quiz.prompt: "I will finish the report tomorrow," he said. → He said he ___ (finish) the report the next day.
quiz.why: "Will" back-shifts to "would" when the reporting verb is past.

quiz.prompt: Direct: "Do you live here?" she asked. Reported:
options: She asked do I live there.
She asked if I live here.
She asked if I lived there.
She asked if did I live there.
quiz.why: Reported yes/no questions use "if" + statement word order, with tense and place words shifted back (live→lived, here→there).

quiz.prompt: "Where do you work?" he asked. → He asked where ___ (I/work).
quiz.why: Reported wh-questions drop "do" and use statement word order, with the tense shifted back.

quiz.prompt: Direct: "Sit down," the teacher said. Reported:
options: The teacher told us to sit down.
The teacher said to us sit down.
The teacher told us sit down.
The teacher said us to sit down.
quiz.why: Reported commands use told + object + to-infinitive.

quiz.prompt: "I can help you," she said. → She said she ___ (can) help me.
quiz.why: "Can" back-shifts to "could" in reported speech.

quiz.prompt: "I'll finish this today," he said. Reported later: He said he would finish it ___.
quiz.why: "Today" shifts to "that day" when the reporting happens at a different time.

quiz.prompt: "You must arrive early," the manager said. → The manager said we ___ (must) arrive early.
quiz.why: "Must" (obligation) back-shifts to "had to" in reported speech.

quiz.prompt: "My car broke down," she said. → She said ___ car had broken down.
quiz.why: The first-person possessive "my" shifts to the third-person "her" when reported by someone else.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite in reported speech: "I have lost my keys," she said.
answers: She said (that) she had lost her keys.
She said she had lost her keys.
She said that she had lost her keys.
She said (that) she had lost her keys
She said she had lost her keys
She said that she had lost her keys
quiz.why: Present perfect back-shifts to past perfect, and "my" shifts to "her".

title: Conditionals 0–3
blurb: Talk about facts, likely futures, hypotheticals and past regrets with if-clauses.

lesson.body: Conditional sentences link a condition (the if-clause) to a result. English has four main patterns, each expressing a different degree of reality or time: general facts, real future possibilities, unreal present situations, and impossible past situations.

lesson.title: Zero & First conditional
lesson.body: Zero conditional (if + present simple, present simple) states a general truth: If you heat ice, it melts. First conditional (if + present simple, will + base) describes a real future possibility: If demand rises, prices will increase.

lesson.title: Second conditional
lesson.body: Use if + past simple, would + base for a hypothetical present or future: If the government invested more, unemployment would fall. In formal or hypothetical style, were replaces was for all persons: If I were you, I would apply.

lesson.title: Third conditional & mixed
lesson.body: Use if + past perfect, would have + past participle for an impossible past: If the policy had been implemented earlier, the crisis would have been avoided. A mixed conditional (if + past perfect, would + base) links a past cause to a present result: If she had studied medicine, she would be a doctor now.

head: Type
If-clause
Main clause
Example
rows: If you freeze water, it turns to ice.
If prices fall, demand will increase.
If I had more time, I would travel more.
If they had invested earlier, they would have profited.

items.en: If you mix red and blue paint, you get purple.
items.note: Zero conditional — a general, always-true fact.

items.en: If the trend continues, prices will double by 2030.
items.note: First conditional — a real future possibility.

items.en: If more people used bicycles, air pollution would fall.
items.note: Second conditional — a present hypothetical.

items.en: If she had left earlier, she would have caught the train.
items.note: Third conditional — an impossible past.

items.en: If she had studied engineering, she would have a very different career now.
items.note: Mixed conditional — a past condition, present result.

lesson.body: For band 7+ Speaking and Writing, the second and third conditionals are often needed to discuss hypothetical solutions (If the government were to invest in public transport, congestion would decrease) and to evaluate past policy (If stricter laws had been introduced, the outcome would have been different).

quiz.prompt: If you ___ water to 100°C, it boils.
quiz.why: A general scientific fact uses the zero conditional: if + present simple, present simple.

quiz.prompt: If the trend ___ (continue), prices will double by 2030.
quiz.why: The if-clause of a first conditional uses the present simple, even though the meaning is future.

quiz.prompt: If it rains tomorrow, the match ___.
quiz.why: A real future possibility takes the first conditional: if + present simple, will + base.

quiz.prompt: If I ___ (be) you, I would reconsider the offer.
quiz.why: The second conditional describes a hypothetical, and "were" (or informally "was") is used with "if I".

quiz.prompt: If she ___ earlier, she would have caught the train.
quiz.why: The third conditional describes an impossible past: if + past perfect, would have + past participle.

quiz.prompt: If the company ___ (invest) sooner, it would have avoided the loss.
quiz.why: The if-clause of a third conditional needs the past perfect.

quiz.prompt: If more people ___ bicycles, air pollution would fall.
quiz.why: The second conditional's if-clause uses the past simple for a hypothetical present situation.

quiz.prompt: If she ___ (study) engineering, she would have a very different career now.
quiz.why: A mixed conditional pairs a past-perfect condition with a present result (would + base).

quiz.prompt: If you mix red and blue, you ___ purple.
quiz.why: A general truth about colour mixing uses the zero conditional, not the first or second.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite as a third conditional: "She didn't warn us, so we made a mistake."
answers: If she had warned us, we wouldn't have made a mistake.
If she had warned us, we would not have made a mistake.
If she had warned us, we wouldn't have made a mistake
If she had warned us, we would not have made a mistake
quiz.why: The unreal past cause and result both take third conditional forms: had warned / would (not) have made.

title: Relative Clauses
blurb: Add detail with who/which/that/whose — and know when commas are required.

lesson.body: A relative clause adds information about a noun using a relative pronoun (who, which, that, whose, where, when). Defining clauses identify which one is meant; non-defining clauses simply add extra, non-essential information.

lesson.title: Defining relative clauses
lesson.body: No commas; the information is essential to the meaning. That or who/which can both be used, and the pronoun can be dropped if it is the object of the clause: The report (that) she wrote won an award.

lesson.title: Non-defining relative clauses
lesson.body: Commas are required; the information is extra and could be removed without changing the core meaning. That cannot be used, and the pronoun cannot be dropped: The Amazon, which covers much of Brazil, is shrinking.

lesson.title: Whose, where, when
lesson.body: Whose shows possession (the woman whose car was stolen); where refers to a place (the town where I grew up); when refers to a time (2010 was the year when the company was founded).

items.en: The scientist who discovered the vaccine won a prize.
items.note: Defining clause — identifies which scientist.

items.en: The Nile, which flows through eleven countries, is the longest river in Africa.
items.note: Non-defining clause — extra information, set off by commas.

items.en: The company whose profits doubled last year is expanding rapidly.
items.note: "Whose" shows possession.

items.en: This is the town where I grew up.
items.note: "Where" replaces "in which" for a place.

items.en: The theory that was proposed in 1990 is now outdated.
items.note: Defining clause with "that" — no commas.

lesson.body: In Writing Task 2, a non-defining clause (...which is a major concern,) lets you add an evaluative comment smoothly inside one sentence. A missing comma before a non-defining clause is a common accuracy error, so check the punctuation as carefully as the pronoun choice.

quiz.prompt: The scientist ___ discovered the vaccine won a prize.
quiz.why: "Who" refers to a person as the subject of the clause.

quiz.prompt: The company ___ profits doubled last year is expanding rapidly.
quiz.why: "Whose" shows that the profits belong to the company.

quiz.prompt: Choose the correctly punctuated sentence (the speaker has only one brother).
options: My brother who lives in Canada is a doctor.
My brother, who lives in Canada is a doctor.
My brother who lives in Canada, is a doctor.
My brother, who lives in Canada, is a doctor.
quiz.why: With only one brother, the clause is non-defining extra information and needs a comma on both sides.

quiz.prompt: The museum ___ hosted the exhibition is in the city centre.
quiz.why: A defining clause identifying the museum can use "that" or "which" as the subject.

quiz.prompt: The woman ___ car was stolen called the police.
quiz.why: "Whose" shows possession (the woman's car), not "who's" (who is/has).

quiz.prompt: 2010 was the year ___ the company was founded.
quiz.why: "When" introduces a relative clause about a time.

quiz.prompt: The Nile, ___ flows through eleven countries, is the longest river in Africa.
quiz.why: This is a non-defining clause (set off by commas), so "that" cannot be used; "which" is correct.

quiz.prompt: This is the town ___ I grew up.
quiz.why: "Where" refers to the place (the town) and replaces "in which".

quiz.prompt: Which sentence is correctly punctuated?
options: The theory, that was proposed in 1990, is now outdated.
The theory that was proposed in 1990 is now outdated.
The theory which was proposed in 1990, is now outdated.
The theory, which was proposed in 1990 is now outdated.
quiz.why: This is a defining clause identifying which theory, so no commas are used and "that" is allowed.

quiz.prompt: Combine using a relative clause: "The professor won an award. She teaches linguistics."
answers: The professor who teaches linguistics won an award.
The professor, who teaches linguistics, won an award.
The professor who teaches linguistics won an award
The professor, who teaches linguistics, won an award
quiz.why: "Who" introduces a clause identifying or adding information about the professor.

title: Modal Verbs
blurb: Express ability, permission, obligation, advice and deduction with modal verbs.

lesson.body: Modal verbs (can, could, may, might, must, should, will, would) add meaning such as ability, permission, obligation, advice or possibility to a main verb. They are followed by the base form, with no -s and no to.

lesson.title: Ability & permission
lesson.body: Can/could express ability or informal permission: Can I ask a question? May expresses more formal permission: May I ask a question? Other tenses use be able to: She hasn't been able to attend.

lesson.title: Obligation & advice
lesson.body: Must shows the speaker's own strong feeling of obligation; have to shows an obligation from an outside rule. Mustn't means prohibition, while don't have to means there is no obligation — a very different meaning. Should/ought to give advice.

lesson.title: Possibility & deduction
lesson.body: May/might/could express possibility; must expresses a confident positive deduction; can't expresses a confident negative deduction: That can't be true.

head: Certainty
Modal
Example
rows: She must be the new manager.

items.en: You mustn't smoke in this building.
items.note: Prohibition, not simply "no obligation".

items.en: You don't have to attend the meeting if you're busy.
items.note: No obligation — very different from mustn't.

items.en: The lights are off and the car is gone — she must have left already.
items.note: Confident positive deduction about the past.

items.en: It might rain later, so take an umbrella just in case.
items.note: Possibility, not certainty.

items.en: Passengers have to show a valid ticket before boarding.
items.note: Obligation from an external rule.

lesson.body: Confusing mustn't (prohibition) with don't have to (no obligation) is a common band-lowering error in IELTS Speaking and Writing when discussing rules or policies. Check the meaning, not just the modal, before you write it down.

quiz.prompt: You ___ smoke in this building — it's strictly forbidden.
quiz.why: "Mustn't" expresses prohibition, matching "strictly forbidden".

quiz.prompt: You ___ (not/have) to attend the meeting if you're busy — it's optional.
quiz.why: "Don't have to" means there is no obligation, matching "optional".

quiz.prompt: The lights are off and the car is gone — she ___ have left already.
quiz.why: The evidence strongly supports one conclusion, so "must" expresses a confident positive deduction.

quiz.prompt: That ___ (can) be right — the figures don't add up.
quiz.why: "Can't" expresses a confident negative deduction.

quiz.prompt: In my opinion, you ___ practise writing every day if you want a higher score.
quiz.why: "In my opinion" signals advice rather than an order, so "should" is correct.

quiz.prompt: She ___ (can) speak three languages by the age of ten.
quiz.why: "Could" expresses general ability in the past.

quiz.prompt: ___ I use your phone, please?
quiz.why: "May" is the standard modal for formally requesting permission.

quiz.prompt: Passengers ___ (have) to show a valid ticket before boarding.
quiz.why: "Have to" expresses an obligation set by an outside rule (the airline/station).

quiz.prompt: It ___ rain later, so take an umbrella just in case.
quiz.why: "Might" expresses possibility, not certainty, matching "just in case".

quiz.prompt: Rewrite using "must" for logical deduction: "I'm sure he is the manager because he has the office keys."
quiz.why: "Must" expresses a confident deduction based on evidence.

title: Gerunds vs Infinitives
blurb: Choose -ing or to + verb after the right verbs, adjectives and prepositions.

lesson.body: Some verbs are followed by a gerund (-ing), some by a to-infinitive, and a few can take either — with a change in meaning. Gerunds also follow prepositions.

lesson.title: Verbs + gerund
lesson.body: enjoy, avoid, suggest, consider, finish, mind, admit, practise + -ing: She suggested revising the essay.

lesson.title: Verbs + to-infinitive
lesson.body: want, decide, agree, plan, hope, afford, manage, refuse + to + base verb: They decided to postpone the meeting.

lesson.title: Verbs that take both, with a meaning change
lesson.body: remember/forget/stop/try + gerund or infinitive change meaning. Stop smoking (quit the habit) vs stop to smoke (pause in order to smoke). Remember doing (a memory of a past act) vs remember to do (not forgetting a future obligation).

head: Pattern
Example
rows: They decided to postpone the meeting.
He is interested in studying abroad.
She is good at solving problems.

items.en: She enjoys learning new languages.
items.note: Verb + gerund.

items.en: They decided to postpone the conference until next year.
items.note: Verb + to-infinitive.

items.en: I remember reading this article last year — it was fascinating.
items.note: Remember + gerund = a memory of a past action.

items.en: Please remember to lock the door before you leave.
items.note: Remember + to-infinitive = don't forget a future task.

items.en: We stopped to have a coffee on the way to work.
items.note: Stop + to-infinitive = paused in order to do something.

lesson.body: Mixing up remember to do and remember doing — or overusing one pattern — signals limited range. For band 7+ Grammatical Range and Accuracy, examiners reward accurate variety across gerund and infinitive patterns.

quiz.prompt: She enjoys ___ new languages.
quiz.why: "Enjoy" is always followed by a gerund.

quiz.prompt: They decided ___ (postpone) the conference until next year.
quiz.why: "Decide" is followed by a to-infinitive.

quiz.prompt: He avoids ___ junk food before exams.
quiz.why: "Avoid" is always followed by a gerund.

quiz.prompt: She is interested in ___ (study) renewable energy.
quiz.why: A preposition ("in") is followed by a gerund, not an infinitive.

quiz.prompt: I remember ___ this article last year — it was fascinating.
quiz.why: "Remember + gerund" refers to a memory of something already done.

quiz.prompt: Please remember ___ (lock) the door before you leave.
quiz.why: "Remember + to-infinitive" means not forgetting a task that still needs to be done.

quiz.prompt: We stopped ___ a coffee for five minutes on the way to work.
quiz.why: "Stop + to-infinitive" means pausing one activity (walking/driving) in order to do another (have a coffee).

quiz.prompt: The government has agreed ___ (increase) funding for public transport.
quiz.why: "Agree" is followed by a to-infinitive.

quiz.prompt: The report suggested ___ the current policy.
quiz.why: "Suggest" is always followed by a gerund, never a to-infinitive.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite so the sentence uses "to + infinitive": "She managed finishing the project on time."
answers: She managed to finish the project on time.
She managed to finish the project on time
quiz.why: "Manage" takes a to-infinitive, not a gerund.

title: Used to / Would / Be used to
blurb: Talk about past habits with used to/would, and present adaptation with be/get used to.

lesson.body: Three similar-looking structures have very different meanings. Used to + base describes a past habit or state that is no longer true. Would + base describes a repeated past action (not a state). Be/get used to + -ing/noun describes familiarity or adapting to a situation, in any tense.

lesson.title: Used to
lesson.body: A past habit or state that is no longer true: I used to live in Hanoi. Questions and negatives use did: Did you use to play tennis? I didn't use to like coffee.

lesson.title: Would (habitual past)
lesson.body: Repeated past actions only, not states such as live, have, know, like, hate: Every summer, we would visit our grandparents. You cannot say "would live" or "would hate" to describe a past state.

lesson.title: Be/get used to
lesson.body: Be used to + -ing/noun means accustomed to something (any tense: is/was/will be used to). Get used to describes the process of becoming accustomed. This has a completely different meaning from "used to do" — a common confusion.

head: Structure
Meaning
Example
rows: She is used to working nights now.

items.en: When I was a child, I would go to the beach every summer.
items.note: Would + repeated action.

items.en: She used to work as a teacher before she became a lawyer.
items.note: Used to + a past job that has changed.

items.en: It took him months to get used to the noise of the city.
items.note: Get used to = the process of adapting.

items.en: After ten years abroad, she is used to eating spicy food every day.
items.note: Be used to = accustomed to, in the present.

items.en: He never used to eat vegetables as a child, but he loves them now.
items.note: Used to + past habit contrasted with the present.

lesson.body: In Speaking Part 1/2, when talking about childhood or past routines, "used to" and "would" add natural range beyond the simple past. Never use "would" for past states — say used to have, not would have, for possession or description.

quiz.prompt: When I was a child, I ___ to the beach every summer.
quiz.why: A repeated past action needs "would" + base verb; the other options misuse the "used to" structure.

quiz.prompt: She ___ (use) to work as a teacher before she became a lawyer.
quiz.why: "Used to" describes a past job that is no longer true.

quiz.prompt: As a teenager, she ___ a large collection of stamps.
quiz.why: "Have" describes a state, and "would" cannot describe past states — only "used to" can.

quiz.prompt: It took him months to ___ (get) used to the noise of the city.
quiz.why: "Get used to" describes the process of becoming accustomed to something.

quiz.prompt: I'm not used ___ early — I usually work night shifts.
quiz.why: "Be used to" is always followed by a gerund (or noun), introduced by "to": to getting up.

quiz.prompt: ___ (do) you use to live near the coast?
quiz.why: Questions with "used to" for past habits are formed with "did" + subject + use to.

quiz.prompt: After ten years abroad, she ___ eating spicy food every day.
quiz.why: "Be used to" + gerund expresses being accustomed to something in the present.

quiz.prompt: Every Friday, my grandfather ___ (would/tell) us stories about the war.
quiz.why: "Would" describes a repeated past action (telling stories), not a state.

quiz.prompt: He ___ vegetables as a child, but he loves them now.
quiz.why: "Hate" is a state verb; "would" cannot describe past states, only "used to" can.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite using "used to": "She smoked when she was younger, but she doesn't anymore."
answers: She used to smoke when she was younger.
She used to smoke when she was younger
quiz.why: "Used to" describes a past habit that is no longer true.

title: Wish & If only
blurb: Express regrets, unreal wishes and complaints about the present with wish/if only.

lesson.body: Wish and if only talk about unreal situations. The tense that follows changes the meaning: a present wish uses the past simple, a past regret uses the past perfect, and a wish about someone's annoying behaviour uses would.

lesson.title: Wishing about the present
lesson.body: Wish/if only + past simple describes something unreal now: I wish I had more time. With be, were is preferred for all persons: I wish I were taller.

lesson.title: Wishing about the past (regret)
lesson.body: Wish/if only + past perfect expresses regret about a past action: I wish I had studied harder. / If only I had accepted the offer.

lesson.title: Wishing for change or complaining
lesson.body: Wish + subject + would + base complains about someone else's (or something's) annoying, repeated behaviour: I wish he would answer his phone. It cannot be used about yourself or an unchangeable state.

head: Use
Form
Example
rows: If only I had accepted the offer.
I wish he would answer his phone.

items.en: I wish I had more free time these days.
items.note: Present unreal wish.

items.en: If only I had studied harder for the exam last year.
items.note: Regret about a past action.

items.en: I wish you would stop leaving your dishes in the sink.
items.note: Complaint about someone else's repeated behaviour.

items.en: If only I were taller — I'd play basketball professionally.
items.note: "Were" is used with "if I" in unreal wishes.

items.en: I wish I could help you, but I have no experience in this area.
items.note: "Could" expresses an unreal present ability.

lesson.body: "If only" adds more emotional emphasis than "wish" for regrets (If only I had prepared more for the interview) — a useful register upgrade for band 7+ fluency and range in Speaking and Writing, as long as the tense rules stay exact.

quiz.prompt: I wish I ___ more free time these days.
quiz.why: A present unreal wish takes the past simple after "wish".

quiz.prompt: She wishes she ___ (can) speak Japanese fluently.
quiz.why: "Wish" + "could" expresses an unreal present ability.

quiz.prompt: If only I ___ harder for the exam last year.
quiz.why: A regret about the past takes the past perfect after "if only".

quiz.prompt: He wishes he ___ (not/miss) the deadline last month.
quiz.why: A regret about a completed past action takes the past perfect.

quiz.prompt: I wish you ___ leaving your dishes in the sink.
quiz.why: A complaint about someone else's repeated, annoying behaviour uses "wish + would".

quiz.prompt: I wish it ___ (stop) raining — we can't start the match.
quiz.why: A complaint about an ongoing, annoying situation uses "wish + would".

quiz.prompt: If only I ___ taller — I'd play basketball professionally.
quiz.why: "If only I" takes "were" for an unreal present state.

quiz.prompt: I wish I ___ (not/eat) so much at lunch — I feel uncomfortable now.
quiz.why: A completed past action causing a present feeling still takes the past perfect after "wish".

quiz.prompt: I wish I ___ help you, but I have no experience in this area.
quiz.why: "Could" expresses an unreal present ability; "would" is only for complaining about others' behaviour.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite using "wish": "I regret not buying that house."
answers: I wish I had bought that house.
I wish I had bought that house
quiz.why: A regret about a past action takes "wish + past perfect".

title: Causatives (have/get something done)
blurb: Say that someone else did something for you with have/get + object + past participle.

lesson.body: The causative structure (have/get + object + past participle) describes an action that someone else performs, arranged or paid for by the subject. I had my hair cut means a hairdresser cut it, not that I cut it myself.

lesson.title: Form
lesson.body: Have/get + object + past participle. The tense changes on have/get: I have my car serviced every year. She had her photo taken. We're getting the roof repaired.

lesson.title: Have vs get
lesson.body: Get is more informal and can emphasise effort in arranging something; have is neutral and more formal. Both express the same causative idea in most everyday contexts.
